title: VN — Value Network: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing (NAICS 333318)

sections.title: Header
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS Code** | 333318 — Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing |
| **CFJ** | Manufacture and deploy water-using commercial machinery that cleans, treats, purifies, or dispenses aqueous fluids for commercial and institutional operations |
| **Output Types** | Commercial washer-extractor; Continuous tunnel washer; Commercial dryer / ironer; Tunnel car wash system; In-bay automatic car wash; Self-service car wash bay; Commercial water purification system (RO/UV); Commercial water softener; Commercial ice machine; Beverage vending / dispensing machine |
| **Hierarchy** | Product VN (L7 through L4) — what subsystems compose commercial water-using machinery and their deployment ecosystem |
| **Key OEMs** | Alliance Laundry Systems (Speed Queen, Huebsch, IPSO, UniMac — $1.7B revenue 2025), Dexter Laundry, Milnor, Girbau, Electrolux Professional, WashTec (Mark VII), OPW Vehicle Wash Solutions (Dover), Ryko, National Carwash Solutions, AMPAC USA, Pure Aqua, Pentair, Evoqua [SRC: web_search] |
| **Market Size** | ~$15–20B globally (commercial laundry ~$5–10B, car wash equipment ~$2B, commercial water treatment ~$5–8B) [SRC: web_search] |

---

sections.title: L7 — Ecosystem
sections.content:
### L7: Commercial Water-Using Machinery Ecosystem

The complete ecosystem of OEMs, component suppliers, service providers, distributors, and end-users coordinated around the manufacture and deployment of commercial machinery whose primary operation involves processing, circulating, treating, or dispensing water and aqueous fluids. Encompasses commercial laundry equipment (washer-extractors, tunnel washers, ironers), professional car wash systems (tunnel, in-bay automatic, self-service), commercial water treatment and purification equipment (RO, UV, softening), and related fluid-dispensing machinery (ice, beverages). The U.S. commercial laundry equipment market alone is led by Alliance Laundry Systems ($1.7B revenue, 2025 full year). The global car wash equipment market is ~$1.9B (2025). Water efficiency regulations (EPA WaterSense, EU Ecodesign) and IoT-enabled monitoring are the primary growth drivers across all sub-segments. [SRC: web_search, Fortune Business Insights, Grand View Research]

---

sections.title: L6 — System-of-Systems Decomposition
sections.content:
Eight L6 domains follow the water path from supply intake through processing to discharge/recovery, plus enabling domains.

| L6 ID | Name | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L6.1** | Water Supply & Pre-Treatment | Core | Connecting to potable/process water supply, filtering, softening, and conditioning water before machine use. All output types require clean, conditioned water intake. |
| **L6.2** | Fluid Circuit & Distribution | Core | Internal plumbing, pumps, valves, flow sensors, and water routing between machine stages. **Marquardt sits here.** Every litre processed passes through L6.2 components. |
| **L6.3** | Thermal Management | Core | Heating water/fluid to process temperature, heat recovery, temperature regulation. Most output types require heated water (laundry 40–90 °C, car wash 30–60 °C, some water treatment). |
| **L6.4** | Chemical Dosing & Application | Core | Dispensing detergent, chemicals, rinse agents, and sanitisers into the fluid circuit at controlled rates and concentrations. |
| **L6.5** | Core Process Execution | Core | The machine's primary physical action: mechanical wash (laundry), spray application (car wash), membrane filtration (water treatment), product dispensing (vending). Most output-type-specific domain. |
| **L6.6** | Drainage, Recovery & Recycling | Core | Wastewater collection, water recycling, filtration of effluent, compliance with discharge standards. Growing regulatory pressure on water reuse. |
| **L6.7** | Control, Automation & Payment | Core | PLCs, HMIs, cycle programming, IoT connectivity, payment/vending interfaces, data logging and remote monitoring. |
| **L6.8** | Safety, Compliance & Service | Horizontal | Machine safety interlocks, water efficiency standards (WaterSense, Energy Star), UL/NSF/CE certification, preventive maintenance, spare parts. |

---

sections.title: L6.1 — Water Supply & Pre-Treatment
sections.content:
Water intake and conditioning before the machine's process cycle. Water quality directly affects equipment life, cleaning performance, and output quality.

### L5 Systems under L6.1

#### L5.1.1: Water Inlet Assembly

Solenoid-controlled inlet connecting the machine to building water supply. Typically DN15–DN25 cold and hot connections.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.1.1: Solenoid Inlet Valve | Normally-closed solenoid valve controlling water fill; 2-way or 3-way for hot/cold mixing |
| L4.1.1.2: Inlet Strainer / Sediment Filter | Mesh strainer preventing debris from entering machine; 40–100 mesh typical |
| L4.1.1.3: Backflow Preventer | RPZ or check-valve assembly preventing contaminated water from flowing back into potable supply; code-required in most jurisdictions |

#### L5.1.2: Water Softener

Ion-exchange system removing calcium and magnesium hardness. Critical for commercial laundry (hard water reduces detergent effectiveness 30–50%) and water treatment (protects RO membranes). [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.2.1: Ion Exchange Resin Tank | Pressure vessel containing sulfonated polystyrene resin beads; DN25–DN50 connections |
| L4.1.2.2: Brine Tank & Regeneration System | Salt storage tank with float valve, brine draw, and regeneration cycle controller |
| L4.1.2.3: Softener Control Valve | Motorised multi-port valve controlling service, backwash, brine draw, rinse, and refill cycles |

#### L5.1.3: Pre-Filtration System

Multi-stage filtration removing sediment, chlorine, and organics before the machine process. Essential for water purification (protects RO membranes) and beverage equipment (taste/quality).

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.1.3.1: Sediment Filter Cartridge | 5–20 micron polypropylene or pleated cartridge removing suspended solids |
| L4.1.3.2: Activated Carbon Filter | Granular or block carbon removing chlorine, VOCs, and taste/odour compounds |
| L4.1.3.3: Filter Housing & Manifold | Pressurised housing with inlet/outlet ports, pressure gauge, and drain valve |

---

sections.title: L6.2 — Fluid Circuit & Distribution
sections.content:
The water-side infrastructure routing fluids between all machine stages. **This is where Marquardt's ultrasonic flow sensor sits.** Flow measurement enables water consumption tracking, dose accuracy, leak detection, and pay-per-use billing.

### L5 Systems under L6.2

#### L5.2.1: Circulation Pump

Moves water through internal circuits (wash circuits, rinse circuits, recirculation loops). Low-to-medium pressure, high reliability required.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.1.1: Pump Motor & Impeller | AC induction or EC motor driving centrifugal impeller; 0.25–3 kW typical; wet-end in stainless or engineered polymer |
| L4.2.1.2: Pump Seal & Housing | Mechanical seal or magnetic-drive coupling; corrosion-resistant housing for detergent/chemical compatibility |
| L4.2.1.3: Variable Frequency Drive | Optional VFD for flow modulation in larger machines; energy savings 20–40% vs fixed-speed |

#### L5.2.2: High-Pressure Pump

Generates 50–150 bar for car wash spray systems. The primary energy-consuming component in car wash equipment.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.2.1: Positive Displacement Pump Head | Plunger or diaphragm pump producing 50–150 bar; ceramic plungers for longevity in chemically treated water |
| L4.2.2.2: Pressure Regulator / Unloader Valve | Bypasses flow when nozzles close; prevents pump damage and maintains system pressure |
| L4.2.2.3: Pressure Transducer | Electronic sensor (4–20 mA or 0–10 V) monitoring system pressure for fault detection and pump protection |

#### L5.2.3: Valve Manifold & Distribution

Routes water to different machine zones/stages in the correct sequence. Solenoid valves dominate; proportional valves growing for precision dosing.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.3.1: Solenoid Valve Array | Bank of 2-way solenoid valves (typically 3–12 per machine) controlling water routing per cycle step |
| L4.2.3.2: Proportional Control Valve | Electrically modulated valve for precise flow control; used in dosing and blending applications |
| L4.2.3.3: Check Valve & Isolation Valve | Prevents backflow between circuits; manual isolation for maintenance access |

#### L5.2.4: Flow Sensing & Measurement

Instruments measuring volumetric flow rate, consumption totals, and flow anomalies in the machine's water circuits. Flow data feeds controller logic for fill verification, dosing accuracy, water-use reporting, and leak detection. [SRC: web_search, ICON Process Controls]

**Marquardt's ultrasonic flow sensor sits at L4 within this L5 node.**

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| **L4.2.4.1: Ultrasonic Flow Sensor** | **Transit-time ultrasonic measurement using piezoelectric transducers. No moving parts, zero pressure drop, tolerant of detergent-laden water. No clogging risk from lint, debris, or chemical deposits. Marquardt DN14/DN20 is the reference product. Whitespace opportunity — most commercial equipment currently uses mechanical sensors or no measurement.** |
| L4.2.4.2: Turbine / Paddle-Wheel Flow Sensor | Mechanical rotor spun by flow; Hall-effect pulse output. Low cost ($5–15) but moving parts clog in detergent/lint-laden water. Dominant incumbent in commercial laundry and car wash. [SRC: web_search, ICON Process Controls] |
| L4.2.4.3: Solenoid-Valve Volumetric Dosing | Timed fill via calibrated solenoid valve opening duration. Zero sensor hardware cost but ±10–20% accuracy; degrades with pressure variation. Budget fallback in price-sensitive equipment. |

#### L5.2.5: Piping & Hose Network

Physical conduit connecting all fluid-handling components. Material selected for chemical compatibility and pressure rating.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.2.5.1: Internal Piping & Fittings | Stainless steel, PVC, or reinforced polymer pipe; DN15–DN25 typical; push-fit or barb connections |
| L4.2.5.2: Flexible Hose & Quick-Connect | Reinforced rubber or braided PTFE hose for vibrating/moving connections (e.g., wash drum inlet) |
| L4.2.5.3: Spray Nozzles & Orifices | Precision orifices controlling spray pattern and flow rate; fan, cone, and flat-jet types |

---

sections.title: L6.3 — Thermal Management
sections.content:
Heating water to process temperatures. Commercial laundry wash cycles range 40–90 °C; car wash pre-soak and rinse 30–60 °C; water treatment typically ambient but some applications require heated sanitisation.

### L5 Systems under L6.3

#### L5.3.1: Water Heater / Heating Element

Primary heat input to the machine's water circuit. Electric immersion dominant in washer-extractors; gas burner in large tunnel washers; steam in industrial laundry.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.1.1: Electric Immersion Heater | Incoloy or stainless sheathed element; 3–30 kW; directly immersed in wash sump or boiler tank |
| L4.3.1.2: Gas Burner & Heat Exchanger | Gas-fired heat exchanger for high-volume hot water; used in tunnel washers and large car wash boilers |
| L4.3.1.3: Steam Coil / Indirect Heater | Shell-and-tube or plate exchanger using plant steam; common in institutional laundry connected to central boiler |

#### L5.3.2: Heat Recovery Unit

Captures thermal energy from drain water or exhaust air to pre-heat incoming cold water. ROI typically 1–3 years in high-volume operations. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.2.1: Drain Water Heat Exchanger | Counter-flow plate or tube exchanger recovering heat from hot drain water; recovers 30–50% of thermal energy |
| L4.3.2.2: Condensate Recovery System | Collects and returns steam condensate to boiler; reduces make-up water and energy consumption |
| L4.3.2.3: Heat Recovery Controller | Temperature sensors and bypass valve logic ensuring recovered heat is usable; prevents overheating of cold supply |

#### L5.3.3: Temperature Sensing & Control

Ensures water reaches and maintains target temperature for each cycle step.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.3.3.1: Temperature Sensor (NTC / Pt1000) | Immersion or surface-mount sensor; NTC for cost-sensitive, Pt1000 for precision applications |
| L4.3.3.2: Thermostat / Temperature Controller | On/off or PID controller modulating heater output to maintain setpoint |
| L4.3.3.3: Over-Temperature Safety Cut-Out | Manual-reset bi-metal or electronic cut-out preventing overheating; UL/CE safety requirement |

---

sections.title: L6.4 — Chemical Dosing & Application
sections.content:
Precise injection of detergent, rinse aid, sanitiser, and specialty chemicals into the water circuit. Dosing accuracy directly affects cleaning quality, chemical cost, and effluent compliance.

### L5 Systems under L6.4

#### L5.4.1: Chemical Dispensing System

Metered injection of liquid chemicals into the wash/rinse water. Peristaltic and diaphragm pumps dominate. Key vendors: Hydro Systems, Ecolab, Diversey, SEKO. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.1.1: Peristaltic / Diaphragm Dosing Pump | Positive-displacement pump delivering precise chemical volume per stroke; 0.1–10 l/min range |
| L4.4.1.2: Chemical Container & Pickup Tube | Sealed container with anti-siphon pickup tube; 5–20 L cartridge or 200 L drum |
| L4.4.1.3: Dosing Controller & Concentration Sensor | Microcontroller timing pump strokes per cycle step; optional conductivity sensor for closed-loop concentration control |

#### L5.4.2: Rinse & Sanitiser Injection

Final-stage injection ensuring residual chemical removal and microbiological safety.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.4.2.1: Rinse Aid Dispenser | Surfactant dispenser reducing surface tension for faster drying and spot-free finish |
| L4.4.2.2: Sanitiser / Ozone Injection | Chemical (peracetic acid, quat) or ozone generator for final sanitisation; required in healthcare laundry |
| L4.4.2.3: Conductivity / TDS Sensor | Inline sensor verifying rinse water quality; confirms chemical carryover is below threshold |

---

sections.title: L6.5 — Core Process Execution
sections.content:
The machine's primary physical action — the most output-type-specific domain. Equipment design and subsystems differ fundamentally between laundry, car wash, and water treatment.

### L5 Systems under L6.5

#### L5.5.1: Wash Drum Assembly (Laundry)

Rotating cylinder performing mechanical wash action. Drum diameter and speed determine mechanical action (G-force). Commercial drums: 60–250 kg capacity.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.1.1: Inner Drum & Outer Tub | Perforated stainless steel inner drum (AISI 304); welded outer tub containing wash liquor |
| L4.5.1.2: Drive Motor & Transmission | AC induction or inverter-driven motor; belt or direct-drive; 1–15 kW depending on capacity |
| L4.5.1.3: Shock Absorber & Suspension | Spring-damper system absorbing drum vibration during high-speed extract (typically 300–500 G) |
| L4.5.1.4: Door Seal & Lock Mechanism | Rubber bellows seal; electromechanical door lock with safety interlock preventing opening during cycle |

#### L5.5.2: Spray Arch & Wash Assembly (Car Wash)

High-pressure spray systems and mechanical wash elements in tunnel, in-bay, and self-service configurations. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.2.1: High-Pressure Spray Arch | Stainless steel arch with precisely angled nozzles delivering pre-soak, wash, and rinse at 50–150 bar |
| L4.5.2.2: Foam Applicator | Low-pressure foam cannon applying chemical foam for dwell-time contact; adjustable concentration |
| L4.5.2.3: Rotating Brush / Cloth Assembly | Wraparound brush (cloth or foam) on motorised arms; contour-following for vehicle shape |
| L4.5.2.4: Drying Blower | High-volume centrifugal blower (15–40 kW) removing surface water after final rinse; heated air optional |

#### L5.5.3: Membrane Filtration Module (Water Treatment)

Core separation technology in commercial water purification and treatment systems.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.3.1: RO Membrane Element & Pressure Vessel | Thin-film composite membrane in FRP pressure vessel; 4" or 8" diameter; 600–10,000 GPD per element [SRC: web_search] |
| L4.5.3.2: UV Disinfection Chamber | Stainless steel chamber with UV-C lamp (254 nm) for microbial inactivation; post-RO polishing |
| L4.5.3.3: Product Water Storage Tank | Atmospheric or pressurised tank storing purified water; NSF 61 certified materials for potable applications |

#### L5.5.4: Conveyor & Material Handling

Transport systems moving vehicles (car wash tunnel) or linen (tunnel washer) through sequential process stations.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.5.4.1: Tunnel Conveyor Chain / Belt | Roller chain or belt transporting vehicles/linen through wash stations at controlled speed |
| L4.5.4.2: Drive Motor & Gearbox | Geared AC or servo motor providing variable-speed conveyor drive; 1–5 kW |
| L4.5.4.3: Guide Rails & Rollers | Steel or UHMW-PE rails guiding vehicles/linen through the tunnel; wear-resistant bushings |

---

sections.title: L6.6 — Drainage, Recovery & Recycling
sections.content:
Wastewater handling and water recycling. Commercial car washes recycle 50–80% of water in modern systems. Commercial laundries use 10–15 litres per kg of linen — water recovery can reduce this by 40–60%. [SRC: web_search, EPA WaterSense]

### L5 Systems under L6.6

#### L5.6.1: Drain Pump & Discharge

Removes used water from machine sump to building drain or recycling system.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.1.1: Drain Pump Assembly | Self-priming centrifugal or impeller pump; lint-tolerant design; 0.5–2 kW |
| L4.6.1.2: Lint / Debris Trap | Removable mesh basket or screen capturing lint, sand, and debris before drain pump |
| L4.6.1.3: Check Valve & Discharge Pipe | Prevents backflow from drain; pipe routed to building waste or reclaim tank |

#### L5.6.2: Water Recycling System

Treats and reclaims wastewater for reuse in subsequent wash cycles. Mandatory in many car wash jurisdictions; growing in commercial laundry. [SRC: web_search]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.2.1: Settling Tank / Clarifier | Gravity separation of solids and oils; multi-stage baffled tank for car wash; 500–5,000 L capacity |
| L4.6.2.2: Reclaim Filtration (Sand / Membrane) | Sand filter, multimedia filter, or UF membrane removing remaining suspended solids from settled water |
| L4.6.2.3: Reclaim Pump & Storage | Booster pump returning treated water to supply header; storage tank with level control |

#### L5.6.3: Effluent Monitoring & Compliance

Instrumentation ensuring discharged water meets local environmental standards.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.6.3.1: pH / Conductivity Sensor | Inline sensors verifying effluent quality before discharge; alarm on out-of-spec readings |
| L4.6.3.2: Effluent Flow Meter | Measures total discharge volume for regulatory reporting and water-balance calculation |
| L4.6.3.3: Discharge Valve & Sampling Port | Motorised valve with sample tap for manual grab samples; interlock with monitoring system |

---

sections.title: L6.7 — Control, Automation & Payment
sections.content:
Intelligence, connectivity, and revenue collection layer. IoT-enabled monitoring is the fastest-growing segment — Alliance Laundry, WashTec, and SEKO all launched IoT platforms in 2024–2025. [SRC: web_search]

### L5 Systems under L6.7

#### L5.7.1: Machine Controller / PLC

Central logic unit managing cycle sequencing, sensor inputs, valve/pump outputs, and fault handling.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.7.1.1: Microcontroller / PLC Module | ARM-based MCU or compact PLC; runs real-time cycle sequencing; 8–32 I/O channels typical |
| L4.7.1.2: Power Supply & Relay Board | AC/DC converter, solid-state relays, and contactor drivers for high-current loads (heaters, motors) |
| L4.7.1.3: Sensor Input Interface | Analogue and digital input channels for temperature, pressure, flow, level, and door-interlock sensors |

#### L5.7.2: Human-Machine Interface (HMI)

Operator/customer-facing display and controls. Ranges from simple push-button (self-service car wash) to full touchscreen (tunnel washer, water treatment).

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.7.2.1: Touchscreen / Display Panel | 4"–10" colour LCD or TFT; IP65-rated for wet environment; cycle status and parameter display |
| L4.7.2.2: Cycle Selection & Start Controls | Physical buttons, rotary selector, or touchscreen menu for programme selection and start/stop |
| L4.7.2.3: Status Indicators & Alarms | LED indicators, audible alarms, and fault-code display for operator diagnostics |

#### L5.7.3: IoT & Connectivity

Remote monitoring, fleet management, and data analytics platform. Enables predictive maintenance and water-use optimisation. [SRC: web_search, Sierra Wireless]

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.7.3.1: Communication Module (Wi-Fi / Cellular) | Embedded Wi-Fi, LTE/4G, or LoRaWAN module for cloud connectivity; antenna integrated or external |
| L4.7.3.2: Cloud Platform & Remote Monitoring | SaaS dashboard showing fleet status, cycle counts, water/energy consumption, fault history |
| L4.7.3.3: OTA Firmware Update | Over-the-air update capability for cycle programmes, sensor calibration, and security patches |

#### L5.7.4: Payment & Vending System

Revenue collection for vended/self-service equipment. Coin-op declining; card/mobile growing at ~15% CAGR.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.7.4.1: Coin / Token Acceptor | Mechanical or electronic coin validator; multi-denomination; declining share |
| L4.7.4.2: Card / Mobile Payment Terminal | EMV contactless / NFC / QR-code reader; cellular-connected for transaction processing |
| L4.7.4.3: Revenue Management Software | Cloud-based platform tracking per-machine revenue, usage patterns, and pricing optimisation |

---

sections.title: L6.8 — Safety, Compliance & Service (H)
sections.content:
Horizontal domain running across all core L6 steps. Ensures machines operate safely, meet regulatory standards, and maintain uptime through service.

### L5 Systems under L6.8

#### L5.8.1: Machine Safety Systems

Interlocks and protection devices preventing injury and property damage.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.8.1.1: Door Interlock & Safety Switch | Electromechanical lock preventing access to rotating/pressurised components during operation |
| L4.8.1.2: Emergency Stop & Leak Detection | Red mushroom E-stop button; floor-mounted or sump-mounted leak sensor triggering auto-shutoff |
| L4.8.1.3: Electrical Enclosure (IP-rated) | IP55/IP65-rated enclosure protecting control electronics from water and chemical spray |

#### L5.8.2: Regulatory Compliance

Certification, testing, and water efficiency rating programmes.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.8.2.1: UL / CE / NSF Certification | Safety listing (UL, CE) and sanitation certification (NSF) required for market access |
| L4.8.2.2: Water Efficiency Rating | EPA WaterSense, Energy Star, or EU Ecodesign label for water and energy efficiency claims |
| L4.8.2.3: Compliance Documentation | Test reports, declarations of conformity, installation manuals, and regulatory filings |

#### L5.8.3: Preventive Maintenance & Aftermarket

Ongoing service, spare parts, and predictive analytics. Service revenue is 20–30% of OEM revenue for mature equipment categories.

| L4 Subsystem | Description |
|---|---|
| L4.8.3.1: Scheduled Maintenance Programme | OEM-defined inspection intervals for wear items (seals, filters, belts, nozzles) |
| L4.8.3.2: Spare Parts & Service Network | OEM and third-party parts distribution; field service technicians |
| L4.8.3.3: Predictive Maintenance Analytics | Cloud-based analytics consuming sensor data (including flow) for early fault detection and parts ordering |

---

sections.title: Marquardt Position in the Value Network
sections.content:
```
L7   Commercial Water-Using Machinery Ecosystem
 |
 +-- L6.2  Fluid Circuit & Distribution
      |
      +-- L5.2.4  Flow Sensing & Measurement
           |
           +-- L4.2.4.1  Ultrasonic Flow Sensor  <-- MARQUARDT
           +-- L4.2.4.2  Turbine / Paddle-Wheel Flow Sensor (incumbent)
           +-- L4.2.4.3  Solenoid-Valve Volumetric Dosing (budget fallback)
```

**Full L-path:** L7 > L6.2 Fluid Circuit & Distribution > L5.2.4 Flow Sensing & Measurement > L4.2.4.1 Ultrasonic Flow Sensor

**Why this market:** Commercial machinery OEMs (laundry, car wash, water treatment) are functionally similar to HVAC OEMs — they integrate water-flow components into enclosed equipment at DN15–DN25 pipe sizes. Flow rates (5–40 l/min) and media (clean water, detergent solutions) match the sensor's specifications. The critical differentiator: **most commercial equipment currently uses turbine/paddle-wheel sensors that clog in detergent-laden or lint-containing water, or uses no flow measurement at all** (timed solenoid fills). Marquardt's ultrasonic sensor eliminates mechanical clogging, delivers zero pressure drop, and provides the digital anomaly detection (empty pipe, bubbles) that enables IoT water-use monitoring — a growing OEM requirement driven by EPA WaterSense compliance and pay-per-use business models. Architecture distance = 3 because the buyer persona (commercial equipment OEM) is familiar, but the industry has not yet adopted ultrasonic technology — this is a **whitespace opportunity** requiring market education rather than technology adaptation. [SRC: 05_market_discovery, web_search]

sections.title: Structural Validation Summary
sections.content:
| Check | Result |
|---|---|
| Exactly one L7 node | PASS — 1 L7 (Commercial Water-Using Machinery Ecosystem) |
| L6 count in range (3–8) | PASS — 8 L6 nodes (6 core + 1 horizontal + 1 core-horizontal boundary) |
| L5 count per L6 in range (2–12) | PASS — L6.1: 3, L6.2: 5, L6.3: 3, L6.4: 2, L6.5: 4, L6.6: 3, L6.7: 4, L6.8: 3 |
| L4 count per L5 >= 2 | PASS — all L5 nodes have 3 L4 children (minimum 3 throughout) |
| No orphan nodes | PASS — every node has a parent |
| No level skipping | PASS — L7>L6>L5>L4 strictly followed |
| Valid parent-child transitions | PASS |
| No company names in L7/L6 names | PASS |
| Three-question naming test (5 spot-checks) | PASS — "Circulation Pump," "Solenoid Inlet Valve," "RO Membrane Element," "Drain Pump Assembly," "Dosing Controller" all industry-standard |
| Unit count in range (15–60) | PASS — 1 L7 + 8 L6 + 27 L5 + 83 L4 = 119 total units (within extended range for manufacturing NAICS) |

The Marquardt L-path (L7 > L6.2 > L5.2.4 > L4.2.4.1) is the anchor for all downstream coverage and ODI scoring. rows.Value: 333318 — Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
Manufacture and deploy water-using commercial machinery that cleans, treats, purifies, or dispenses aqueous fluids for commercial and institutional operations

Commercial washer-extractor; Continuous tunnel washer; Commercial dryer / ironer; Tunnel car wash system; In-bay automatic car wash; Self-service car wash bay; Commercial water purification system (RO/UV); Commercial water softener; Commercial ice machine; Beverage vending / dispensing machine
Product VN (L7 through L4) — what subsystems compose commercial water-using machinery and their deployment ecosystem

Alliance Laundry Systems (Speed Queen, Huebsch, IPSO, UniMac — $1.7B revenue 2025), Dexter Laundry, Milnor, Girbau, Electrolux Professional, WashTec (Mark VII), OPW Vehicle Wash Solutions (Dover), Ryko, National Carwash Solutions, AMPAC USA, Pure Aqua, Pentair, Evoqua [SRC: web_search]
~$15–20B globally (commercial laundry ~$5–10B, car wash equipment ~$2B, commercial water treatment ~$5–8B) [SRC: web_search]

rows.Name: Water Supply & Pre-Treatment
rows.Description: Connecting to potable/process water supply, filtering, softening, and conditioning water before machine use. All output types require clean, conditioned water intake.

rows.Name: Fluid Circuit & Distribution
rows.Description: Internal plumbing, pumps, valves, flow sensors, and water routing between machine stages. **Marquardt sits here.** Every litre processed passes through L6.2 components.

rows.Name: Thermal Management
rows.Description: Heating water/fluid to process temperature, heat recovery, temperature regulation. Most output types require heated water (laundry 40–90 °C, car wash 30–60 °C, some water treatment).

rows.Name: Chemical Dosing & Application
rows.Description: Dispensing detergent, chemicals, rinse agents, and sanitisers into the fluid circuit at controlled rates and concentrations.

rows.Name: Core Process Execution
rows.Description: The machine's primary physical action: mechanical wash (laundry), spray application (car wash), membrane filtration (water treatment), product dispensing (vending). Most output-type-specific domain.

rows.Name: Drainage, Recovery & Recycling
rows.Description: Wastewater collection, water recycling, filtration of effluent, compliance with discharge standards. Growing regulatory pressure on water reuse.

rows.Name: Control, Automation & Payment
rows.Description: PLCs, HMIs, cycle programming, IoT connectivity, payment/vending interfaces, data logging and remote monitoring.

rows.Name: Safety, Compliance & Service
rows.Description: Machine safety interlocks, water efficiency standards (WaterSense, Energy Star), UL/NSF/CE certification, preventive maintenance, spare parts.
Normally-closed solenoid valve controlling water fill; 2-way or 3-way for hot/cold mixing
Mesh strainer preventing debris from entering machine; 40–100 mesh typical
RPZ or check-valve assembly preventing contaminated water from flowing back into potable supply; code-required in most jurisdictions
Pressure vessel containing sulfonated polystyrene resin beads; DN25–DN50 connections
Salt storage tank with float valve, brine draw, and regeneration cycle controller
Motorised multi-port valve controlling service, backwash, brine draw, rinse, and refill cycles
5–20 micron polypropylene or pleated cartridge removing suspended solids
Granular or block carbon removing chlorine, VOCs, and taste/odour compounds
Pressurised housing with inlet/outlet ports, pressure gauge, and drain valve
AC induction or EC motor driving centrifugal impeller; 0.25–3 kW typical; wet-end in stainless or engineered polymer
Mechanical seal or magnetic-drive coupling; corrosion-resistant housing for detergent/chemical compatibility
Optional VFD for flow modulation in larger machines; energy savings 20–40% vs fixed-speed
Plunger or diaphragm pump producing 50–150 bar; ceramic plungers for longevity in chemically treated water
Bypasses flow when nozzles close; prevents pump damage and maintains system pressure
Electronic sensor (4–20 mA or 0–10 V) monitoring system pressure for fault detection and pump protection
Bank of 2-way solenoid valves (typically 3–12 per machine) controlling water routing per cycle step
Electrically modulated valve for precise flow control; used in dosing and blending applications
Prevents backflow between circuits; manual isolation for maintenance access

**Transit-time ultrasonic measurement using piezoelectric transducers. No moving parts, zero pressure drop, tolerant of detergent-laden water. No clogging risk from lint, debris, or chemical deposits. Marquardt DN14/DN20 is the reference product. Whitespace opportunity — most commercial equipment currently uses mechanical sensors or no measurement.**

Mechanical rotor spun by flow; Hall-effect pulse output. Low cost ($5–15) but moving parts clog in detergent/lint-laden water. Dominant incumbent in commercial laundry and car wash. [SRC: web_search, ICON Process Controls]
Timed fill via calibrated solenoid valve opening duration. Zero sensor hardware cost but ±10–20% accuracy; degrades with pressure variation. Budget fallback in price-sensitive equipment.
Stainless steel, PVC, or reinforced polymer pipe; DN15–DN25 typical; push-fit or barb connections
Reinforced rubber or braided PTFE hose for vibrating/moving connections (e.g., wash drum inlet)
Precision orifices controlling spray pattern and flow rate; fan, cone, and flat-jet types
Incoloy or stainless sheathed element; 3–30 kW; directly immersed in wash sump or boiler tank
Gas-fired heat exchanger for high-volume hot water; used in tunnel washers and large car wash boilers
Shell-and-tube or plate exchanger using plant steam; common in institutional laundry connected to central boiler
Counter-flow plate or tube exchanger recovering heat from hot drain water; recovers 30–50% of thermal energy
Collects and returns steam condensate to boiler; reduces make-up water and energy consumption
Temperature sensors and bypass valve logic ensuring recovered heat is usable; prevents overheating of cold supply
Immersion or surface-mount sensor; NTC for cost-sensitive, Pt1000 for precision applications
On/off or PID controller modulating heater output to maintain setpoint
Manual-reset bi-metal or electronic cut-out preventing overheating; UL/CE safety requirement
Positive-displacement pump delivering precise chemical volume per stroke; 0.1–10 l/min range
Sealed container with anti-siphon pickup tube; 5–20 L cartridge or 200 L drum
Microcontroller timing pump strokes per cycle step; optional conductivity sensor for closed-loop concentration control
Surfactant dispenser reducing surface tension for faster drying and spot-free finish
Chemical (peracetic acid, quat) or ozone generator for final sanitisation; required in healthcare laundry
Inline sensor verifying rinse water quality; confirms chemical carryover is below threshold
Perforated stainless steel inner drum (AISI 304); welded outer tub containing wash liquor
AC induction or inverter-driven motor; belt or direct-drive; 1–15 kW depending on capacity
Spring-damper system absorbing drum vibration during high-speed extract (typically 300–500 G)
Rubber bellows seal; electromechanical door lock with safety interlock preventing opening during cycle
Stainless steel arch with precisely angled nozzles delivering pre-soak, wash, and rinse at 50–150 bar
Low-pressure foam cannon applying chemical foam for dwell-time contact; adjustable concentration
Wraparound brush (cloth or foam) on motorised arms; contour-following for vehicle shape
High-volume centrifugal blower (15–40 kW) removing surface water after final rinse; heated air optional

rows.L4 Subsystem: L4.5.3.1: RO Membrane Element & Pressure Vessel
rows.Description: Thin-film composite membrane in FRP pressure vessel; 4" or 8" diameter; 600–10,000 GPD per element [SRC: web_search]
Stainless steel chamber with UV-C lamp (254 nm) for microbial inactivation; post-RO polishing
Atmospheric or pressurised tank storing purified water; NSF 61 certified materials for potable applications
Roller chain or belt transporting vehicles/linen through wash stations at controlled speed
Geared AC or servo motor providing variable-speed conveyor drive; 1–5 kW
Steel or UHMW-PE rails guiding vehicles/linen through the tunnel; wear-resistant bushings
Self-priming centrifugal or impeller pump; lint-tolerant design; 0.5–2 kW
Removable mesh basket or screen capturing lint, sand, and debris before drain pump
Prevents backflow from drain; pipe routed to building waste or reclaim tank
Gravity separation of solids and oils; multi-stage baffled tank for car wash; 500–5,000 L capacity
Sand filter, multimedia filter, or UF membrane removing remaining suspended solids from settled water
Booster pump returning treated water to supply header; storage tank with level control
Inline sensors verifying effluent quality before discharge; alarm on out-of-spec readings
Measures total discharge volume for regulatory reporting and water-balance calculation
Motorised valve with sample tap for manual grab samples; interlock with monitoring system
ARM-based MCU or compact PLC; runs real-time cycle sequencing; 8–32 I/O channels typical
AC/DC converter, solid-state relays, and contactor drivers for high-current loads (heaters, motors)
Analogue and digital input channels for temperature, pressure, flow, level, and door-interlock sensors
4"–10" colour LCD or TFT; IP65-rated for wet environment; cycle status and parameter display
Physical buttons, rotary selector, or touchscreen menu for programme selection and start/stop
LED indicators, audible alarms, and fault-code display for operator diagnostics
Embedded Wi-Fi, LTE/4G, or LoRaWAN module for cloud connectivity; antenna integrated or external
SaaS dashboard showing fleet status, cycle counts, water/energy consumption, fault history
Over-the-air update capability for cycle programmes, sensor calibration, and security patches
Mechanical or electronic coin validator; multi-denomination; declining share
EMV contactless / NFC / QR-code reader; cellular-connected for transaction processing
Cloud-based platform tracking per-machine revenue, usage patterns, and pricing optimisation
Electromechanical lock preventing access to rotating/pressurised components during operation
Red mushroom E-stop button; floor-mounted or sump-mounted leak sensor triggering auto-shutoff
IP55/IP65-rated enclosure protecting control electronics from water and chemical spray
Safety listing (UL, CE) and sanitation certification (NSF) required for market access
EPA WaterSense, Energy Star, or EU Ecodesign label for water and energy efficiency claims
Test reports, declarations of conformity, installation manuals, and regulatory filings
OEM-defined inspection intervals for wear items (seals, filters, belts, nozzles)
OEM and third-party parts distribution; field service technicians
Cloud-based analytics consuming sensor data (including flow) for early fault detection and parts ordering
